Title: Empire Premium bets on affordability to drive growth amid rising costs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: As cost pressures intensify across Malaysia's food and beverage sector, Empire Premium Food Bhd, the operator of the Empire Sushi chain, is doubling down on affordability as a key competitive edge to drive growth.
Co-founder Nicole Lim said the company's long operating track record has enabled it to navigate ongoing geopolitical disruptions and cost volatility, supported by a flexible menu strategy that helps cushion fluctuations in input costs.
Positioned as a value-for-money brand, Empire Sushi has carved out a niche by offering more affordable options compared with many other sushi chains.
The company's pricing strategy, supported by its grab-and-go-based model and streamlined operations, allows it to keep costs relatively low while maintaining accessibility for mass-market consumers, she said after the company's prospectus launch on Wednesday.
She added that the company's focus on affordability, combined with consistent product quality, has enabled it to appeal to a broad customer base, particularly as consumers become more selective in their spending.
However, she stressed that pricing alone is not sufficient to sustain demand in an increasingly competitive market.
"Consumers are particular about taste, as even at a lower price point, products must meet expectations," she said.
Ahead of its debut on the Main Market of Bursa Malaysia next month, Empire Premium has set its initial public offering (IPO) price at 70 sen per share.
The listing is expected to raise RM152.6 million for the company, alongside RM96.3 million for its co-founders, husband-and-wife team Jordan Tan and Lim, according to the company's prospectus.
Of the proceeds, RM79.1 million, or 51.8 per cent, will be channelled towards expanding 56 new outlets nationwide, while RM12.6 million (8.3 per cent) will be used to refurbish existing outlets. Another RM52.1 million (34.1 per cent) has been earmarked for working capital, with the remaining RM8.8 million allocated for IPO-related expenses.
The IPO comprises 218 million new shares and 145 million existing shares, bringing the total offering to 363 million shares.
Meanwhile, Lim said the company has long operated in a challenging cost environment and has built resilience through a structured yet flexible business model developed over more than a decade.
"We have faced many cost increases over the past 12 years, but one of our strengths is our ability to adapt. Every quarter, we review and replace items that do not perform well with new products that can encourage higher demand among consumers," she said.
Beyond revenue optimisation, the company places strong emphasis on cost control at the outlet level, maintaining an average wastage rate of about 7.0 per cent. This is supported by standard operating procedures that regulate production volumes and product display timing throughout the day.
Lim noted that the company aims to strike a balance between minimising waste and ensuring product availability, particularly during peak periods.
"A lower wastage rate may not necessarily be better if it affects product availability and sales. We aim to balance both revenue and cost efficiency," she said.
Looking ahead, the company has identified workforce development as a key operational challenge as it scales post-listing.
Given the skill-intensive nature of sushi preparation, training and retaining staff will be critical to maintaining quality and consistency across its expanding network, Lim said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Contrarian investing success
Article:
CONTRARIAN investing is an investment strategy that involves going against prevailing market sentiment. While most investors tend to follow trends—buying when prices are rising and selling when they are falling—contrarian investors deliberately do the opposite. They seek opportunities where assets are undervalued due to excessive pessimism and avoid or sell assets that are overvalued due to excessive optimism. At its core, contrarian investing is rooted in the belief that markets are often driven by emotion rather than rational analysis, leading to mispricing that disciplined investors can exploit.
The philosophy behind contrarian investing is closely tied to the idea that the crowd is frequently wrong at extremes. When markets are euphoric, prices can be pushed far above intrinsic value, creating bubbles. Conversely, during periods of fear or panic, prices can fall well below their true worth. Contrarian investors aim to capitalise on these inefficiencies by maintaining independence of thought and resisting the psychological pressures that influence the majority.
One of the primary reasons to adopt a contrarian approach is the potential for superior long-term returns. By buying undervalued assets when others are selling, investors can acquire quality investments at a discount. When sentiment eventually shifts and prices recover, these investments can generate significant gains. This approach aligns with the principle of "buy low, sell high" but requires the courage and patience to act when it feels most uncomfortable.
Another compelling reason to be a contrarian investor is the opportunity to avoid herd behaviour, which is a common cause of investment losses. Many investors are influenced by cognitive biases such as fear of missing out (FOMO) and loss aversion. These biases often lead to poor decision-making, such as buying at market peaks or selling during downturns. Contrarian investors, by definition, resist these impulses. They rely on independent analysis, focusing on fundamentals such as earnings, cash flow, and intrinsic value rather than short-term market movements.
Contrarian investing also benefits from the concept of mean reversion, which suggests that asset prices tend to move back toward their historical averages over time. When prices deviate significantly from their intrinsic value due to market sentiment, they are likely to correct eventually. Contrarians position themselves to benefit from this reversion, buying when prices are abnormally low and selling when they are excessively high.
However, being a contrarian investor is not simply about doing the opposite of the crowd at all times. Blindly opposing market trends can be just as risky as blindly following them. Successful contrarian investing requires careful analysis and a strong understanding of the underlying value of an investment. It is important to distinguish between assets that are undervalued due to temporary factors and those that are fundamentally flawed. Not every declining stock is a bargain, and not every popular investment is overvalued.
Patience is another critical attribute for contrarian investors. Markets can remain irrational longer than expected, and it may take time for the true value of an investment to be recognised. During this period, contrarian investors must be able to withstand short-term losses and negative sentiment. This requires emotional discipline and a long-term perspective, as well as confidence in one's analysis.
Additionally, contrarian investing can provide diversification benefits. By investing in assets that are out of favour, contrarians often hold positions that are less correlated with the broader market. This can help reduce overall portfolio risk and enhance returns over time. It also encourages a more thoughtful and deliberate approach to investing, as decisions are based on analysis rather than trends.
There are also broader strategic advantages. Contrarian investors often uncover opportunities in neglected sectors or regions that are overlooked by mainstream investors. These areas may offer higher growth potential or better valuations precisely because they are underappreciated. By venturing into these less crowded spaces, contrarians can gain an edge over the competition.
Despite its advantages, contrarian investing is not without challenges. It can be psychologically difficult to go against the crowd, especially during periods of strong market momentum. There is also the risk of being early rather than wrong—buying an undervalued asset that continues to decline before recovering. This underscores the importance of thorough research, risk management, and diversification.
In conclusion, contrarian investing is a disciplined strategy that seeks to exploit market inefficiencies created by human behaviour. By going against prevailing sentiment, contrarian investors aim to buy undervalued assets and avoid overpriced ones, thereby achieving superior long-term returns. The approach requires independent thinking, patience, and emotional resilience but offers significant rewards for those who can adhere to its principles. In a market often driven by fear and greed, the contrarian investor stands apart, guided not by the crowd but by conviction and careful analysis.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Markets await Middle East stability for sustained gains
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A clearer sign of normalisation in the Middle East is needed for the local equity market to post a sustainable positive momentum, said economists.
They said the global equities market continues to depend on the trajectory of the conflict in Iran, which is still largely uncertain at this stage.
Tradeview Capital fund manager Neoh Jia Man said the market is pricing in a relatively swift resolution to the Middle East conflict within the coming weeks.
"Should this expectation not materialise, prolonged disruptions could lead to shortages and elevated raw material costs across multiple industries. This would likely weigh on corporate margins and broader economic activity, posing downside risks to the Malaysian equity market," he told Business Times.
He said that the sustainability of the current market momentum depends on the development of the ongoing conflict.
"We would look for clearer signs of normalisation, particularly a recovery in vessel traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, before turning more constructive on the market outlook," he added.
Asian equities rose on Wednesday, and currencies rebounded as oil tumbled after Washington claimed that it was seeking a month-long ceasefire with Iran.
This development eased fears of prolonged supply disruptions and offered relief to Asia's oil-importing economies.
Bursa Malaysia opened higher on Wednesday, supported by improved risk appetite across regional markets despite a weaker overnight performance on Wall Street.
The key index opened 13.74 points higher at 1,722.50 and continued its upward momentum throughout the trading period.
It closed 7.92 points higher at 1,716.68.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said US President Donald Trump's inconsistent lack of exit plans for the war has led to greater economic uncertainties.
"The closure of the Straits of Hormuz along with the destruction of oil and gas facilities in the Gulf region would mean the negative supply shock from the petroleum and liquefied natural gas sector will raise the cost of energy.
"Policy makers are scrambling to deal with such blowouts, as the crisis will likely sap the purchasing power among consumers and businesses," he said.
STOCKS IN FOCUS
Meanwhile, Afzanizam said that there are pockets of opportunities in the equities market amid the ongoing war in Iran.
Defensive sectors such as healthcare as well as companies in renewable energy could be the main beneficiaries.
"As such, fund managers are likely to be tactical in their trading ideas, with most of them preferring to stay in cash in order to preserve their investment principle," he said.
Neoh said investor positioning currently reflects divergent views on the conflict's progression, with oil & gas, chemicals and plantation sectors seen as beneficiaries. On the flipside, consumer staples, consumer discretionary and transportation are perceived as laggards.
"That said, we see merit in looking beyond these crowded trades. Sectors such as utilities, renewable energy and financials, where earnings fundamentals are relatively more resilient and valuations have already corrected, may offer a more attractive risk-reward profile for investors," added Neoh.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil price outlook: Q2 dip before rising on Middle East risk, says Fitch
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Oil prices are expected to topple from an average of US$78 per barrel (bbl) in the first quarter (Q1) to US$66/bbl in Q2 before climbing higher later in the year, according to BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions.
The firm kept its forecast for Brent crude to set at an annual average of US$70/bbl for this year.
"This aligns with our country risk team's base-case scenario of a four-week conflict and assumes a relatively rapid post-conflict recovery, with limited lasting damage to critical upstream production and export infrastructure and a two-to-four-week normalisation of flows through the Strait of Hormuz," it said in a note.
However, the firm noted the rising risk of a longer-duration war, assigning a 35 per cent probability to their 'extend-to-end' scenario, which lasts up to eight weeks, and a 25 per cent probability to their 'extend-to-escalate' scenario, lasting multiple months.
"If we move into the 'extend-to-end' scenario, we will raise our annual average forecast for Brent into a range of US$75-82/bbl for 2026."
BMI said it does not believe the disconnect between paper and physical markets can be sustained and expects greater contagion to Brent prices under the 'extend-to-end' scenario.
It also sees global market deficits ranging from 7.5 million to 15 million barrels per day.
"Market participants and policymakers have been pulling on a lot of levers to offset the losses from the Gulf, including pipeline rerouting away from the strait, strategic inventory releases, a drawdown of oil on water and commercial stocks on land, a temporary waiver of certain oil-related sanctions on Russia (and, to lesser effect, Iran), and an easing of some domestic fuel regulations," it added.
However, it said these buffers are rapidly being depleted and there will be far fewer levers to pull in the next four weeks compared to in the previous four.
"Moreover, as the war continues, the threats to residual flows through the Strait of Hormuz and to the volumes transiting the Red Sea will rise, as will the risk of wider-spread damage to critical production and export infrastructure in the region, which could slow the post-conflict recovery," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fatal LaGuardia incident underscores risks of runway incursions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A runway collision involving an Air Canada Express regional jet and a fire truck at New York's LaGuardia Airport on March 22 has drawn attention to runway safety procedures and air traffic control coordination.
Various media reports said the incident, in which the two pilots were killed and dozens of passengers and emergency personnel injured, is being treated as runway incursion.
This is when an aircraft, vehicle or person is incorrectly present on a runway.
Early reports indicate both the Air Canada Express aircraft and the fire truck may have received clearance to use the runway.
A pilot familiar with airport operations said runway incursions were among the most serious operational risks in aviation, especially during landing when aircraft were still travelling at speed but had minimal ability to manoeuvre.
"During landing, the aircraft is still moving fast and you cannot just stop the aircraft on the runway immediately. If something appears ahead of you, there is very little time to react," he said.
He said pilots relied on air traffic control to ensure the runway was clear once landing clearance was issued.
"When you are cleared to land, that means the runway is cleared or protected, so to speak, for you. Pilots operate on the assumption that no vehicles or aircraft will enter that runway," he added.
Runway incursions have long been identified by aviation regulators as a major safety concern, particularly at busy airports where aircraft and ground vehicles operate in close proximity.
The pilot said emergency situations often increase operational complexity because rescue vehicles such as fire trucks may be despatched quickly sometimes while aircraft are still taxiing or landing.
"In an emergency, everything moves very quickly. Fire trucks are despatched immediately, and they need to reach the location as fast as possible.
"But even then, there are strict procedures. The runway must be secured, or controllers must ensure there is no conflicting traffic before any vehicle enters," he said.
He added that visibility from the cockpit during the landing roll, particularly at night, could be limited.
While runway incursions are rare, they are considered high-risk events because they can lead to severe accidents, particularly if they occur during take-off or landing.
"Air traffic control, pilots and ground vehicles all operate in the same environment. Safety depends on everyone having the same situational awareness at all times," the pilot said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Weak enforcement of fireworks sales sparks safety fears
Article:
news@nst.com.my
KUALA LUMPUR: A safety activist has sounded the alarm over the mushrooming of fireworks stalls in public spaces, warning that weak enforcement and poor safety practices could lead to a tragedy.
K. Bala Supramaniam said many such stalls appeared to be set up without proper consideration for safety, citing unsafe demonstrations in crowded areas, including pedestrian walkways and shoplots.
He said traders were operating directly in front of business premises, including restaurants where patrons often smoke outside, next to the stalls selling fireworks.
Bala, who founded the Malaysian Volunteer Fire and Rescue Association, urged authorities to impose stricter regulations, including limiting fireworks sales to open spaces away from ignition sources such as people, vehicles and cigarettes.
"Fireworks stalls should not be located near hospitals, clinics, schools or areas storing gas cylinders, as these locations pose heightened risks and could disrupt essential services.
"We do not want another tragedy like the Sungai Buloh Bright Sparklers fireworks factory explosion. Approval must not be given in critical areas," he said.
He was referring to the 1991 disaster, which caused 26 deaths, 103 injuries and destroyed 46 homes.
With hot and humid weather further increasing fire risks, Bala said it was crucial for traders to be equipped with fire extinguishers and adhere strictly to safety protocols.
He called for tighter controls on storage, suggesting that traders should only be allowed to keep minimal stock to reduce the risk of fires spreading.
He also suggested that all traders undergo fire safety training conducted by the Fire and Rescue Department and be certified and inspected before being granted permits.
With Hari Raya Aidilfitri celebrations lasting up to a month, he urged the authorities to step up monitoring and enforcement, including regular inspections by fire officials.
"The department already has the authority to stop hazardous activities. They must act if these stalls pose a danger."
Meanwhile, Alliance for a Safe Community chairman Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye said those involved in selling fireworks in public places should take a proactive approach to safety.
"There is no harm in keeping fire extinguishers readily available. In the event of a fire, they can act immediately to douse it and prevent it from spreading.
"As a precaution, local authorities should also issue regular reminders about fire safety. Fireworks are commonly used during festive seasons and this makes safety awareness even more important," he said.
Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming had said standard operating procedures  for the sale of fireworks at temporary stalls would be reviewed.
He  said the Fire and Rescue Department and the Local Government Department had  been directed to conduct the review.
Calls have been mounting for tighter controls or a total ban on the sale of firecrackers and fireworks following a string of accidents and concerns over safety, weak enforcement and the proliferation of illegal products.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Experts: Urn thefts inflict deep emotional, cultural harm on families
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The theft of over  50 urns is not just a property crime, but also  a case of "weaponising grief", said experts, warning that existing laws fail to capture the full extent of the harm inflicted.
While the offence may fall under Section 380 of the Penal Code for theft in a building, a senior criminologist said the legal classification underrepresents the qualitative impact of the crime.
Datuk Dr P. Sundramoorthy said such cases went far beyond conventional property offences, carrying deep psychological, cultural and emotional consequences.
He said Section 380 addressed the act of theft primarily in material terms, overlooking the profound symbolic harm involved.
"The theft effectively weaponises grief, causing distress, a sense of violation and prolonged disruption to mourning," he told the
New Straits Times
, describing the issue as a form of "expressive victimisation", where emotional harm outweighs material loss.
He said one plausible motive was instrumental opportunism, where offenders believed urns might contain valuables that could be resold.
"In such cases, the ashes are incidental, with offenders targeting perceived economic gain in low-surveillance areas such as cemeteries or columbaria."
He said this aligned with routine activity theory, where offenders exploited poorly guarded spaces.
But he added that economic motives alone were insufficient to explain the nature of the crime.
He said offenders must engage in moral disengagement, often trivialising the contents of the urns or distancing themselves from their human significance.
He said the ransom demand worsened the offence, shifting it from opportunistic theft to coercive exploitation. "By demanding payment, the offender is amplifying emotional harm to extract financial gain, reflecting elements of extortion and psychological manipulation."
He said current laws might not fully capture the gravity of such offences, calling for the recognition of crimes involving human remains as aggravating factors in sentencing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops probe possible link in urn theft cases at three memorial parks
Article:
KULAI: Police are investigating whether there is a link in the theft of urns from three columbaria following the arrest of four suspects and the recovery of four urns in the past month.
The suspects — two Malaysians and two foreigners — were picked up in connection with the theft of 22 urns from the Perpetual Memorial Park in Kulai, Johor. They were released on bail.
Similar thefts were also reported at the Xiao En Memorial Park in Nilai, Negri Sembilan, where 30 urns were stolen, and at the Nirvana Memorial Park.
Nilai police recovered four urns and recorded statements from 10 witnesses, but no arrests have been made.
Kulai police chief Assistant Commissioner Tan Seng Lee said police had recorded statements from 23 people, including family members and memorial park staff.
He declined to elaborate when asked if police were looking into the possibility of insider involvement, only saying that investigations were ongoing.
The incident in Kulai was uncovered on March 6 when the memorial park assistant manager noticed that several urns were missing from the rack at Perpetual Memorial Park in Jalan Kota Tinggi-Kulai.
Nilai police chief Superintendent Johari Yahya said investigators were working to complete witness statements and verify the identities of the ashes in the recovered urns.
He said police were looking at all angles, including the possibility of insider involvement.
"This is still at an early stage and cannot be confirmed at this point."
Nirvana Memorial Park officials  claimed that they recovered some stolen urns, but declined to give details.
The theft of more than 50 urns from the three colambaria have shocked families and the community.
The Federation of Chinese Cemetery Associations Malaysia chairman Lee Koon Lam had said the thefts could be the work of "insiders", as the nature of the thefts suggested they were carefully planned with inside help.
"It is not easy to remove urns from a columbarium. There are locks, access points and controlled movement involved.
"It is impossible to pull off such a theft without someone from inside providing information or assistance," he had told the
New Straits Times
.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN names African slave trade 'gravest crime against humanity'
Article:
UNITED NATIONS: The UN General Assembly on Wednesday designated the transatlantic African slave trade as "the gravest crime against humanity," in a move advocates hailed as a step towards healing and possible reparations.
The resolution was adopted to applause by a vote of 123 in favour, three against – the United States, Israel and Argentina – and 52 abstentions, including Britain and member states of the European Union.
"The transatlantic slave trade was a crime against humanity that struck at the core of personhood, broke up families, and devastated communities," UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said.
"To justify the unjustifiable, slavery's proponents and beneficiaries constructed a racist ideology – turning prejudice into a pseudoscience."
Ghana's President John Mahama, one of the African Union's most vocal supporters of slavery reparations, was at the United Nations headquarters to support the vote.
The resolution went beyond simple acknowledgment, asking nations involved in the slave trade to engage in restorative justice.
"Today, we come together in solemn solidarity to affirm truth and pursue a route to healing and reparative justice. The adoption of this resolution serves as a safeguard against forgetting," said Mahama.
The resolution declared "the trafficking of enslaved Africans and racialized chattel enslavement of Africans as the gravest crime against humanity."
The text also highlighted the legacy of slavery via "the persistence of racial discrimination and neo-colonialism" in today's society. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Integrity at stake: Calls to revoke titles for convicted individuals
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The revocation of federal and state awards, medals and honours should be seriously considered when a title holder is found guilty of a crime to protect the dignity of the award.
Majlis Datuk Dato Malaysia (MDMM) executive committee member Datuk Dr Tock Min Kin said titles were conferred in recognition of honour, trust and service to the nation and not merely ceremonial distinctions.
He said they symbolised leadership, integrity and responsibility.
"Honours carry moral weight and those who accept them must uphold exemplary conduct.
"MDDM upholds the principle of presumption of innocence. However, in cases involving serious criminal charges, we believe a formal review mechanism should be triggered pending the outcome of court proceedings.
"Upon conviction, revocation should be seriously considered by the conferring authority to protect the dignity of the award."
Last year, His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia warned that he would withdraw federal awards, medals and honours from anyone who commits a crime, tarnishes the country's image or is punished by the courts.
He said they were chosen for the awards and honours and should be honest, trustworthy, mindful of their conduct and not be involved in corruption or crime.
Recently, several title holders have been charged with crimes, including corruption, while some have been convicted.
Tock said while the constitutional prerogative to confer or revoke titles rested with the king, rulers and Yang di-Pertua Negeri, MDDM believed that the  framework should be strengthened to ensure greater accountability by title holders.
He said the group also supported clearer codes of conduct for title holders.
"We support a structured and transparent review mechanism as well as stronger enforcement under Act 787 to prevent misuse of titles."
He said where deliberate misuse, deception or conduct that brings disrepute to the honours system was proven, stricter penalties, including heavier fines and custodial sentences, should be considered as a deterrent.
"The integrity of the honours system must be safeguarded."
Tock said the Emblems and Names (Prevention of Improper Use) Act 1963 already provided for legal action against the improper use and misrepresentation of titles and honours.
However, he said, MDDM believed enforcement must be consistent to preserve public confidence in the honours system.
[[nid:1403755]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Experts push for clearer title revocation mechanisms
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Clearer and more consistent mechanisms are needed to suspend or revoke honorary titles, especially when recipients are implicated in serious crimes such as corruption.
This is to protect the integrity of the honours system and maintain public trust, said Malaysian Integrity and Governance Society president Datuk Seri Dr Akhbar Satar.
He said people found guilty of crimes, particularly offences involving corruption and breaches of integrity, should have their titles revoked to protect the reputation of the institutions that conferred them.
However, he said, this should be done only after the person had been convicted and had exhausted all avenues of appeal, in line with the principle of innocent until proven guilty.
"There should also be consensus and the consent of the Malay rulers so that there is better standardisation and coordination."
Transparency International Malaysia president Raymon Ram said when title holders were implicated in serious criminal offences, particularly corruption
or conduct that undermined integrity, the public might reasonably perceive that the honour itself had been diminished.
He said when recipients of honorary titles face criminal allegations or convictions, the reputational impact extended beyond the person and could also weaken public confidence in the honours system.
He called for stronger and more consistent standards, clearer public understanding of the nomination process and credible post-award accountability mechanisms.
"In practice, nomination procedures often involve background screening and integrity checks.
"However, the recurring emergence of controversies involving title holders suggests there may be gaps in consistency, transparency and risk management across jurisdictions."
Raymon said a well-structured framework should clearly distinguish between temporary suspension and permanent revocation.
"Suspension can serve as a precautionary measure when a title holder faces serious criminal charges, ensuring that the integrity of the honours system is preserved while legal proceedings take their course.
"Revocation, on the other hand, may be considered following a conviction or other formally established findings that are incompatible with the values associated with the honour.
"Such an approach will help ensure that decisions are guided by clear principles rather than ad hoc responses to public pressure.
"More importantly, consistent guidelines will protect honourable recipients whose contributions genuinely merit recognition."
He said any reform must respect Malaysia's constitutional framework.
"The conferment of honours is the prerogative of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong at the federal level and of the respective rulers or Yang di-Pertua Negeri at the state level. As such, reforms should focus on strengthening administrative processes rather than altering the constitutional authority behind the honours system."
He said transparency and accountability could be improved through several practical measures, including clearer published criteria for nominations, consistent integrity screening procedures and structured review mechanisms based on established findings from courts or competent authorities.
He said any review process should incorporate procedural fairness, including notice to the person concerned and an opportunity to respond.
"The objective is not to politicise the honours system, but to reinforce its credibility.
"By strengthening governance around nominations, reviews and possible revocations, the honours framework can continue to command public respect while remaining fully aligned with constitutional prerogatives and the principles of due process."
[[nid:1403747]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Brazil unveils first locally made supersonic fighter jet
Article:
GAVIAO PEIXOTO: Brazil on Wednesday unveiled the first supersonic fighter jet to be produced locally, placing it among a small group of countries able to build such advanced aircraft.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva christened the F-39E Gripen fighter jet from the Swedish group Saab with champagne during a high-profile ceremony in Gaviao Peixoto in the state of Sao Paulo.
The Brazilian presidency said the building of the supersonic jet was "unprecedented in Latin America."
"This project allows us to consolidate our deterrent power by enhancing our capacity to ensure national sovereignty and regional security," Brazil's Defense Minister Jose Mucio said during the ceremony.
Brazil has ordered 36 supersonic fighter jets from Saab, 15 of which will be manufactured at the facilities of the Brazilian aerospace manufacturer Embraer in Gaviao Peixoto.
Brasilia opted for the Swedish aircraft over the Rafale from France's Dassault Aviation and the F/A-18 Super Hornet from US company Boeing.
The deal -- awarded in 2014 -- was plagued with corruption allegations. Lula was accused of receiving around one million dollars to help Saab win the tender during his first term in office.
However investigations in Brazil and Sweden were closed with no evidence of wrongdoing found by Saab, while the case against Lula was dismissed.
Brazil has highlighted the importance of boosting defense capabilities in a turbulent geopolitical landscape.
Lula has criticized the war against Iran waged by the United States and Israel.
He has also denounced Washington's actions in Latin America, saying the US attack on Venezuela to oust president Nicolas Maduro crossed "an unacceptable line."
During an official visit by South African President Cyril Ramaphosa earlier this month, Lula warned both nations were at risk of foreign invasion.
"I don't know if comrade Ramaphosa realizes that if we don't prepare ourselves in terms of defense, one day someone will invade us," said Lula. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: S. Korean 'drug lord' extradited from the Philippines
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
SEOUL: South Korean police yesterday took into custody an alleged "drug lord" accused of running a narcotics ring in the country from a prison in the Philippines, said officials.
Park Wang-yeol, who was serving a 60-year term for triple homicide, was flown to South Korea on temporary extradition, after President Lee Jae-myung requested the handover to Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr at a summit meeting earlier this month.
The temporary clause under a treaty between the two countries halts the sentence in the Philippines to allow the suspect to be investigated in South Korea, said officials from the foreign and justice ministries, here.
South Korean authorities had said Park, believed to be 47, operated an alleged ring smuggling "large quantities" of illegal narcotics and conspiring with accomplices in the country to distribute them.
Justice Ministry and police officials declined to comment on the scale or value of Park's alleged drug trade.
Some South Korean media reported he had been distributing about 60kg of methamphetamine a month with a street value of 30 billion won.
Illegal drug use in South Korea has steadily grown despite a tough anti-drugs policy and crackdown on illicit drug imports and sales.
Lee thanked Marcos for Park's extradition and said the country "will chase anyone harming the country to the end of the earth".
It was important to ensure Park was investigated and face trial in South Korea so his alleged involvement in illegal activities while under incarceration abroad does not become an example for potential copycat criminals, said the Foreign Ministry.
Park had broken out of the Philippines prison twice, taking advantage of inadequate inmate supervision that allowed some prisoners to use smuggled mobile phones and continue illegal activities, said the Justice Ministry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Musk's SpaceX looking to raise US$75b in June IPO
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
NEW YORK: SpaceX, the company founded by tech billionaire Elon Musk, will soon file for an initial public offering in June that could raise US$75 billion to fund its space exploration plans, a report said on Tuesday.
Citing a person with knowledge of the plan, tech news site The Information said the IPO prospectus would be filed with securities regulators this week or next.
SpaceX, which is developing a spacecraft for reaching Mars, was valued at US$1.25 trillion in early February, when it merged with Musk's artificial intelligence unit xAI.
In recent months, analysts have expected Musk would seek to raise between US$30 billion and US$50 billion, given strong investor demand for technology firms.
If the IPO achieves its target, it would surpass the previous largest offering on record: Saudi Aramco's US$25.6 billion IPO in 2019.
The company did not immediately respond to an AFP request for comment.
SpaceX, which dominates the space launch market with its reusable rockets, remains privately held by Musk and a range of investors, including some funds connected to Google's parent, Alphabet.
Analysts have said that taking SpaceX public will require it and Musk to provide greater financial transparency, particularly about its revenues. It could also increase pressure to demonstrate profitability.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Water cuts dampen Raya cheer for Gua Musang folk
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
Constant water cuts have dampened the Hari Raya celebrations for residents in Tanah Putih, here.
Galas assemblyman Datuk Mohd Syahbuddin Hashim said he had received numerous complaints, adding that the problem worsened ahead of the Hari Raya weekend.
He said homes in higher grounds had faced disruptions for seven months, with the conditions deteriorating recently.
"The problem became more pronounced during Hari Raya, with families lacking water for bathing and cooking."
Syahbuddin said water tankers provided only short-term relief.
"I urge the authorities to undertake immediate and long-term measures to address the issue."
Tanah Putih resident Nur Hidayah Ramli, 35, said the family had to buy boxes of mineral water for daily use amid the water shortage.
"We were very disappointed when the water ran out. I hope that the issue will be resolved soon," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'El Nino may make 2026 hottest on record'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
A climatology and climate change expert is expecting global temperatures to hit a new high this year, driven by the El Nino phenomenon and global warming.
Professor Emeritus Dr Fredolin Tangang said 2024 was previously the hottest year on record, following the average 1.55 °Celsius increase.
The Academy of Sciences Malaysia fellow said while global temperatures dipped slightly last year due to the La Nina phenomenon (the cool phase in the global climate cycle), it was still the third hottest on record.
The Climate Prediction Centre in the United States has forecast that El Nino will begin between July and August, and intensify towards year end and early 2027, before weakening from March.
"Based on forecasts, the probability of El Nino emerging during the southwest monsoon season exceeds 70 per cent," said Fredolin.
El Nino refers to a condition of extreme environmental warming, where sea surface temperatures rise above average.
"Signs of El Nino occur when the thermocline layer in the eastern Pacific Ocean weakens, reducing the upwelling of cooler water to the sea surface. This rise in sea temperatures triggers the onset of El Nino," he said.
"So far, these signs have just begun to appear on the sea surface in the eastern Pacific Ocean. We are likely to face a strong El Nino event this year."
He added that the dry conditions would increase the risk of drought, forest fires and haze.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Melaka police detain mother, four others over baby skeleton discovery
Article:
MELAKA: A 19-year-old woman is among five individuals detained so far to assist in the investigation into the discovery of a
baby's skeleton
at a house compound in Kelemak, Alor Gajah, today.
Melaka Police Chief Datuk Dzulkhairi Mukhtar said the woman is believed to be the mother of the baby, whose birth was concealed.
He added that those arrested, aged between 16 and 40, include the woman's boyfriend, her mother, sister, and brother.
"All of them, including the woman believed to have given birth to the baby and her boyfriend, were detained shortly after the police received a report," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 318 of the Penal Code for concealing a birth.
Earlier, media reports stated that police found the baby's skeleton, believed to have been born about four months ago, buried at the compound of a house in Kelemak.
The discovery shocked residents of the single-storey terrace home, who were still in the midst of celebrating Syawal.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-junior hockey captain builds RM40mil business empire
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Starting with a business capital of just RM30, he now possesses business assets worth approximately RM40 million.
Such is the determination of the former national junior hockey captain, Muhammad Haziq Samsul, whose entrepreneurial efforts since his teenage years have paid off; he can now take pride in owning Bottol Garage Empire, a business offering motorcycle repair services as well as the sale of spare parts and accessories.
Haziq shared that his first venture was selling snacks while studying at the Bukit Jalil Sports School (SSBJ) with a capital of RM30, and every penny of profit was saved to open a larger business while he simultaneously focused on hockey.
From there, Haziq tried various types of businesses, including opening a barbershop and selling second-hand clothes and helmets, but nothing quite clicked until he made a final, determined attempt by opening Bottol Garage Empire when the country was struck by the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020.
Since then, Haziq - who has competed in prestigious tournaments including the 2013 Junior World Cup and the 2014 Commonwealth Games - has never looked back, now boasting nine Bottol Garage Empire branches across the country.
"From having just one staff member on the first day, I now employ 120 staff across these nine branches. The costs I have to cover for a single month, which includes staff wages and premises rent, are estimated to be between RM600,000 and RM700,000.
"On average, monthly sales are estimated at RM3 million. Last year, we managed to achieve sales of about RM30 million. So, if someone offered me RM40 million to take over this business, I would let it go. That RM40 million is the current value of my business," he said when met.
Haziq said that even he could hardly believe he has achieved such success in the business world at just 32 years of age.
"Perhaps God has given me the opportunity to provide employment opportunities for others. If I wanted to be a successful hockey player, I had to make it happen. It is the same in business; if I don't succeed as a businessman, I only have myself to blame," he said.
Regarding future plans, Haziq said he does not want to rush into expanding the business by adding more branches.
"I just want to maintain the existing branches but improve the service. If we provide the best possible service, people will certainly continue to support our business," he said.
Haziq noted that he still treasures his achievements in hockey, especially during his 10 years representing the national squad.
However, he added that as athletes, they must have a solid plan for what they will do once they are no longer involved in or required by the world of sports.
"My advice to athletes is to find where your strengths or interests lie. If there is a need to study or join specific courses, take them.
"At the very least, when we are no longer needed, we already have something to do for the next chapter of our lives," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man detained over Facebook post insulting Prophet Muhammad
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police detained a 47-year-old local man suspected of insulting Prophet Muhammad (PBUH) over a Facebook post that recently went viral.
Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said a report was received at 1.30am last Sunday regarding the offensive post uploaded by an account using the name 'Sakti Sakti Sakti'.
He said the suspect was arrested by a police team at 8.32pm yesterday in Butterworth, Penang.
"A Vivo 29T mobile phone believed to have been used to upload the post was also seized," he said in a statement today.
He added that a three-day remand order has been obtained from today until March 27 to facilitate investigations.
The case is being investigated under Section 298A of the Penal Code for causing disharmony or enmity on religious grounds, Section 505(c) of the Penal Code for statements made with intent to incite, and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 for improper use of network facilities.
Fadil advised the public not to upload or share content touching on religious, racial or royal sensitivities (3R) that could disrupt national harmony.
"Firm action without compromise will be taken against any individual who violates the law," he said.
– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Luxury Paris hotel near Elysee reopens after fire forces brief closure
Article:
PARIS: A five-star hotel near the Elysee presidential palace in Paris reopened on Wednesday after a blaze earlier forced around 400 people to evacuate the building, the French capital's firefighters and the hotel said.
Two members of staff were slightly injured as they tried to put out the fire which started at around 1020 GMT in the basement kitchen of the 114 Faubourg restaurant, owned by the Bristol hotel, often used by visiting world leaders close to the Elysee, the French president's official residence.
The fire has been fully contained and extinguished, the Bristol hotel said in a statement later on Wednesday, adding that the hotel had been reopened.
Police had closed Faubourg Saint‑Honore street, while more than 100 firefighters were on the scene, the local fire service said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: ETS and KTM Komuter between Batu Caves and Pulau Sebang disrupted
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Keretapi Tanah Melayu Berhad (KTMB) has announced disruptions on its electric train service (ETS) and KTM Komuter after an overhead line fault at KM 434.42 between Batang Benar and Labu stations.
The fault affected the Batu Caves - Pulau Sebang -Batu Caves route, prompting service adjustments to ease passenger travel.
ETS train EP9532 from JB Sentral to KL Sentral will now end at Seremban, with shuttle buses provided to KL Sentral and intermediate stations.
Trains EP9537 and EP9579 from KL Sentral to JB Sentral will transport passengers by bus to Gadut, where they can resume their ETS journey.
KTM Komuter services are also affected. Train 2047 from Batu Caves to Pulau Sebang will carry passengers by bus from Batang Benar to Seremban.
Train 2049 will end at Batang Benar, train 2051 at Labu, and train 2055 at Bangi, with bus transfers provided to continue their trips. Services from Pulau Sebang to KL Sentral will now terminate at Seremban, while trains from KL Sentral towards Pulau Sebang will end at Bangi until further notice.
Passengers not continuing their journey may claim a full refund at ticket counters.
KTMB's technical teams are working to restore normal services as quickly as possible.
Commuters are advised to plan their travel and check KTMB's official channels for updates.
The company apologised for the inconvenience and thanked passengers for their patience.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Seremban riders rush woman through Raya traffic to hospital for childbirth [WATCH]
Article:
SEREMBAN: A man received a remarkable act of kindness from six motorcyclists while rushing his wife to Hospital Tuanku Jaafar (HTJ) in Seremban for childbirth on the third night of Hari Raya.
The moving experience was shared by Muhamad Syafiq Abdullah, 25, on social media after he became trapped in heavy traffic during the urgent journey.
Muhamad Syafiq and his wife, Auni Balqis Mohd Izan, 24, had just arrived at his in-laws' home in Chembong, Rembau, at around 8.30pm on Monday.
"I had just come from Ipoh, Perak, and while unloading our belongings, my wife suddenly said she was in labour," he recalled.
"I rushed back into the car to take her to the hospital. The roads were jammed, and I was anxious, but I had no choice."
